title: Vincetoxicum coriaceum
taxonomy: Vincetoxicum coriaceum is classified within the family Asclepiadaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as milkweed or dogbane. This classification has been revised to Convolvulaceae in some sources. Further, the species is placed in the genus Vincetoxicum, which includes several other species of milkweed. Vincetoxicum coriaceum is native to parts of Asia and Europe. The classification of Vincetoxicum coriaceum is subject to ongoing research and may be refined as more information becomes available.
uses: Vincetoxicum coriaceum, also known as Manchurian swallowwort, is a plant with a rich history of traditional medicinal use in various cultures. In traditional Chinese medicine, it is valued for its potential to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems. The plant's roots and stems are often used to make medicinal preparations, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Vincetoxicum coriaceum for medicinal purposes, but it remains a topic of interest in the field of ethnopharmacology. Its potential medicinal uses are thought to be related to its content of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids.

intro: Vincetoxicum coriaceum, also known as the leathery swallowwort or Vincetoxicum, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, as well as its ability to reduce stress and improve cardiovascular health. The bioactive constituents of Vincetoxicum coriaceum include vincetoxine, a diterpenoid lignan, and other related compounds, which have been shown to exhibit bioactivity. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly used to prepare teas, infusions, and extracts, which are often used to treat a range of conditions, including anxiety and hypertension.
